title: What An American thinks about England

transcript.text: All right, this video is what I think about England.
England was the first country that I traveled to, other than Mexico.
I did that.
I actually arrived in England three days after Princess Diana died, and I was there just like a week.
I rode my bike across it.
Experienced London and up to the north.
England is, some countries have like spiritual energy for me.
You can feel the spiritual energy.
And England's one of those countries where I could really feel like spiritual energy, like good spiritual energy, like a magical place.
Like maybe, what are some other countries like that?
Like Australia was like that.
Nepal was like that.
But, you know, I mean, you get that from countries that you're new to.
So, you know, maybe it was just because it was the first place I went to.
But it's like really green, really green country.
I guess because it rains a lot.
But I didn't rain once the whole time I was there.
Really, like, it's like one big city compared to what I'm used to.
Well, I guess it's kind of like how the Front Range is now.
But the whole country is kind of like, you know, one city to the next city.
You're never really out of a city, out of a town.
The one Hamlet is like five miles after the last one.
Until you get up north and then you have a little bit more space.
But as far as the English people, what do I think about English people?
English people, and I knew this before I went there, but they have lots of different accents.
And it's kind of like I didn't, I only knew that because I was an English major and I studied English.
And I knew there was Cockney and there was like Queen's English.
But there's like lots of different English accents, which is interesting.
Even amongst the one town of London, which is really intriguing for an American to know.
Because you can barely tell the difference between America and the whole country of America.
There's like, you know, a couple of accents.
Like three or four or five accents.
You know, and a lot of those are hard to distinguish.
So anyways, that was kind of interesting.
But I didn't talk to anybody really because I was just riding my bike.
But I did go to a bar and some guy goes at the bar like, what do you think about England?
Like we kind of look of ourselves as kind of like the burnt out light.
Like we used to own half the world.
And I've met actually lots of English people traveling.
They're big travelers.
Like the biggest travelers ever.
Like half the people you meet traveling around the world in whatever given country are from London.
And they kind of have that idea.
Kind of like, oh, we used to own half the world.
You know, like what do we know, you know.
But then they're always like, yeah, but we speak English.
You don't have to learn any other language and stuff.
But they're nice.
As far as like what their impressions of Americans are.
I'll include that in every single country I critique.
English people like Americans in my experience.
Although I have met some English people who travel who didn't like me.
Who don't seem to like Americans.
But there seems to be like kind of two kinds of English.
There's like the cool English.
Who's like hip to the groove and fun to hang out with.
And then you definitely have like sheltered English.
I guess maybe how you would imagine like an American.
Like just square English.
Who are boring and would probably tend to not like me.
Just because I'm American.
And yeah, kind of grumpy.
I did talk to some like Polish girl bartender who's like,
I don't like you.
What are you doing in England?
The people here are grumpy.
It's all dismal.
So maybe if you live there, you're going to pick up on some of that.
And maybe it's brought on by the weather because it rains so much.
And it's really cold up north.
But I found that they're really nice people.
And they're really like friendly people.
Like polite friendly people.
Like you know, not like bouncing off the walls.
Like they are in like Brazil or Morocco or Indonesia.
Like you know, or well anywhere in London.
You can like talk to them and they'll listen to you.
And they'll let you finish talking before they start talking.
And then like you can actually have like a real conversation with grounded people.
London was weird because I got lost in London.
I like rode my bike in a circle in London.
I was trying to go north and I passed some roundabout.
And then next thing I know, 20 minutes later, I passed the same roundabout.
But yeah, there's roundabouts all over the place in England.
But there's lots of thorns.
This isn't just England, but Europe in general.
There's freaking thorns everywhere.
Like if you want to like go off the side of the road to camp.
Or even pee.
You have to like, you know, do yoga, ninja, freaking kung fu flexibility to get yourself through the thorns.
And they like start right on the side of the road.
So like if you want to sleep, you got to be like, you got to like crawl on your hands and knees, you know, under the thorns.
And there's like no thorns at all where I'm from.
Like no thorns at all.
It's awesome.
And I was getting a lot of flats too because of that.
But so that's a bummer for them.
But like bike touring there is awesome because there's lots of like small frontage roads and like that I could take, you know.
Like I guess you're not even loud on the highway, but the roads are really like narrow and, you know, not too much traffic and pretty, you know, countryside.
It's a cool place.
There's definitely a bar scene, like a pub scene there that I found really interesting.
Like you go into a pub and it seems like everybody knows each other.
And it seems like everybody goes in there like every night because people are just like really mellow and they know each other.
And they're not like all like in party mode.
But they're kind of like in like people hanging out in the living room mode.
It's kind of like somebody's house.
Like cozy.
It's like like in America, if you go to a bar, it's like loud and people are talking and it's crowded.
And, you know, all the people there, you know, they're not there every single night.
But like England, there's so many pubs.
They're everywhere that the pubs aren't crowded.
There's just a few people in there who know each other, you know.
And I actually felt out of place in a few of them.
Like they were all staring at me like, who's this guy in our pub type of a thing.
But, you know, nobody said, nobody ever tried to talk to me or anything.
But nobody was like weird to me or anything like that.
It's kind of the same thing in France too.
Only difference is France, if somebody walks in, they shake everybody's hand, including yours.
And then they kiss each other like the father.
I saw father kiss his son, like I think even on the lips.
But it's basically my impression of England.
And I did feel like sadness in the air.
I don't know if it was just me like being away from home for the first time.
Or maybe I was generally picking that up because I was there the whole week that they were mourning Princess Diana.
I actually saw Princess Diana's casket.
But I pulled on the side of the road and I'm like, what's everybody dressed in black for?
And they're like, Princess of Wales.
And I was like, really?
And I waited there like a half hour.
And next thing I know, like the purse dries right in front of me.
And I throw a flower on top of it.
I was like in Northampton upon Wales or whatever where she's from.
But that's basically what I think about England.
I went up into Scotland for an afternoon and riding.
And the Scottish people are weird because they like look at you.
They're like old and they look at you and they go.
And I was like, are they like telling me I'm crazy for being a biker?
Or is that how they say hello?
Do they shake their heads to say hello?
I don't know.
It was just kind of weird.
But that's what I think about England.
England people traveling though, I always get along with them great.
They're really fun-loving and like high energy and, you know, fun to hang out with, talkative.
They remind me of Israelis.
It's just like fun to hang out with.
And they'll hang out with like each other, but they'll hang out with anybody else too.
That's kind of like how Israelis are.
I know they don't say that about Israelis, but my experiences of Israelis are easy to hang out with.
Maybe because I'm American and they like Americans.
But anyways, that's what I think about England.
